text: VO2max declines approximately 7% during the first 21 days after stopping endurance training.
VO2max stabilizes after approximately 56 days of detraining at a level 16% below trained values.

After 84 days of detraining, previously trained athletes still maintain a higher VO2max than sedentary controls (50.8 vs 43.3 ml/kg/min), indicating that fitness is not fully lost even after prolonged inactivity.
The initial decline in VO2max during early detraining is primarily caused by reduced stroke volume, while later declines are driven by reduced arteriovenous oxygen difference.
Skeletal muscle capillarization does not decline with detraining and remains approximately 50% above sedentary levels even after 84 days of inactivity.

Muscle oxidative enzyme activity (citrate synthase, succinate dehydrogenase) declines with a half-time of approximately 12 days after stopping training but stabilizes at levels 50% above sedentary controls.
Stroke volume during exercise declines during early detraining to levels not different from sedentary controls, representing the fastest-lost cardiovascular adaptation.
